compatibility_statement: Compatible with Epic's published FHIR R4 CapabilityStatement (Epic February 2026 release, fetched 2026-04-26). Not affiliated with or endorsed by Epic Systems Corporation. Trademarks of Epic Systems Corporation are used for identification only.

phase_b_findings.description: Editorial summary of the most consequential behaviors observed during the Phase B sweep (2026-04-26). All raw evidence in tests/golden/epic/phase-b-2026-04-26/.

headlines.finding: Unknown FHIR resource types return IIS HTML 404, not a FHIR OperationOutcome
headlines.evidence: GET /FakeResourceTypeThatDoesNotExist?_count=1 → HTTP 404 with text/html body containing '<title>404 - File or directory not found.</title>'. The URL never reaches Epic's FHIR layer; IIS rejects at the routing tier.
headlines.integrator_impact: Defensive parsers that assume every Epic FHIR response is JSON will throw. Wrap all responses in JSON-decode-with-fallback when targeting Epic.

headlines.finding: Silently-ignored search params return HTTP 400 with severity=information
headlines.evidence: GET /Patient?totally-bogus-param=foo → HTTP 400 with two OperationOutcome.issue entries: (1) severity=information, code=invalid, diagnostics='Unknown parameter: TOTALLY-BOGUS-PARAM. Parameter has been ignored.' and (2) severity=fatal, code=business-rule because Epic also requires demographics-or-_id for Patient search. The unknown param itself is informational; the request fails for a different reason.
headlines.integrator_impact: An integrator who reads only the first OperationOutcome.issue or only the HTTP status will misdiagnose the failure. The 'Parameter has been ignored' pattern is a class of silent-ignore bug — Epic accepts your typo'd or misspelled search param and returns results that ignore your intent without surfacing the issue at error severity.

citations.label: FHIR R4 § Search — handling unknown parameters

headlines.finding: Vital-signs Observation page returns 1000 entries on first page
headlines.evidence: GET /Observation?patient={id}&category=vital-signs returned a Bundle with 1000 entries on page 1 and an unambiguous rel=next link. Walked 2 pages successfully; next-page tokens were stable (no duplicate URLs).
headlines.integrator_impact: Epic's default page size is large compared to FHIR norms (~50). Naive clients that load entire result sets into memory will hit memory pressure on prolific resources (vitals, labs, observations). Use _count to cap explicitly. Pagination URL is parseable query-string format, not opaque — clients can inspect it but should not modify it.

headlines.finding: Patient.extension(us-core-birthsex) and Patient.extension(us-core-genderIdentity) absent for canonical sandbox patient
headlines.evidence: Epic's CapabilityStatement declares us-core-patient profile support, but the canonical sandbox Patient (eq081-VQEgP8drUUqCWzHfw3 / Derrick Lin) does not emit either extension. 24 of 28 Patient must-support paths are present; these two and Patient.name.suffix and Patient.name.period are absent.
headlines.integrator_impact: Cannot determine from this single sample whether Epic doesn't implement these extensions or whether Derrick Lin's record simply lacks the data. Need a wider patient set to disambiguate. Recorded as 'missing' in element_deviations with that caveat.

headlines.finding: OperationOutcome uses Epic-proprietary coding system OID 1.2.840.114350.1.13.0.1.7.2.657369
headlines.evidence: Both 404 and 400 OperationOutcomes attached a coding from system 'urn:oid:1.2.840.114350.1.13.0.1.7.2.657369' (Epic Systems' OID arc). Example codes: 59008 ('FHIR ID provided was not found'), 59100 ('Content invalid against the specification or a profile'), 59159 ('content/operation failed to pass a business rule').
headlines.integrator_impact: Vendor-specific coding requires per-vendor mapping if you want to surface human-readable error messages from Epic responses. The standard FHIR severity/code fields are populated correctly so generic error handling still works; only fine-grained categorization needs Epic-aware logic.

headlines.finding: Lab return values DO exist on the sandbox — but only on specific patients, not the canonical Derrick Lin
headlines.evidence: Multi-patient sweep (2026-04-26): Camila Maria Lopez (erXuFYUfucBZaryVksYEcMg3) returns 5 lab Observations (LOINC 4548-4 Hemoglobin A1c plus an Epic-OID code 1558024 paired); Jason Argonaut (eD.LxhDyX35TntF77l7etUA3) returns 7 labs. Derrick Lin's Observation?category=laboratory returns Bundle.total=0. Lab observations carry Observation.code as a CodeableConcept with both LOINC and an Epic-proprietary OID coding (system urn:oid:1.2.840.114350.1.13.0.1.7.2.768282).
headlines.integrator_impact: An integrator testing only with Derrick Lin will incorrectly conclude Epic does not return labs. Multi-patient catalog is mandatory for honest conformance claims. Epic's lab observations dual-code with LOINC + an Epic-proprietary OID per coding (vendor-specific code mirrored alongside the standard); pick the LOINC coding for portable parsing.

headlines.finding: Observation.category:us-core slice carries vendor-proprietary OID coding alongside the required us-core code
headlines.evidence: Sandbox lab Observations include both required `('http://terminology.hl7.org/CodeSystem/observation-category', 'laboratory')` AND vendor-proprietary `('urn:oid:1.2.840.114350.1.13.0.1.7.10.798268.30', 'Lab')` codings on the us-core-clinical-result-observation-category required-bound slice. Same dual-coding pattern observed across vital-signs, encounter type, document reference type, procedure code, and condition code.
headlines.integrator_impact: Required-binding compliance is satisfied (the VS-required code IS present), but receivers MUST scan all coding[] entries — not just coding[0] — to find the conformant code. Code-system-aware filters that pin to LOINC/SNOMED/CIDR will work; positional code[0] readers risk parsing the Epic OID as the canonical clinical code.

citations.label: US Core 6.1 — Required and Extensible bindings

headlines.finding: Condition?category=health-concern returns zero entries for every test patient swept
headlines.evidence: Sweep across all 7 hydrated sandbox patients (Derrick Lin, Camila Lopez, Jason/Jessica Argonaut, Desiree Powell, Linda Ross, Warren McGinnis): Bundle.total=0 with a `mode=outcome` 'no results' OperationOutcome entry. Captured as `Condition-health-concern` skip in sweep summary.
headlines.integrator_impact: us-core-condition-problems-health-concerns supports the `health-concern` category slice in profile but no Epic sandbox patient surfaces health-concern category data. Build/test against a real customer instance to verify behavior; sandbox cannot validate this code path.

citations.label: US Core 6.1 — Condition Problems & Health Concerns

headlines.finding: Bundle.total reports the page size, not the result count, when _count is set
headlines.evidence: GET /Observation?patient={id}&category=laboratory&_count=1 returns Bundle.total=1 even when 5+ matches exist. Adding _summary=count returns Bundle.total=5 with no entry array. Without _count, Bundle.total accurately reports the unbounded match count up to ~1000 (vital-signs cap). Re-validated 2026-05-02 against Derrick Lin's vital-signs feed: `&_count=5` → Bundle.total=5 (entry=5), walking rel=next → Bundle.total still 5 (entry=5), `&_count=1000` → Bundle.total=1000 (entry=1000). Captured in `tests/golden/epic/phase-b-2026-04-26/observation-vital-signs-paginated-with-total-2026-05-02.json`.
headlines.integrator_impact: Integrators that page with _count and trust Bundle.total for progress estimation are misled by this Epic-specific quirk — Bundle.total reflects the page contents, not the result set. Use _summary=count for accurate match counting before walking pages.

headlines.finding: Empty-result Bundles carry a 'mode=outcome' OperationOutcome entry, not an empty entry array
headlines.evidence: GET /Patient?family=NoSuchName&given=NoSuchName returns Bundle{total:0, entry:[{search:{mode:outcome}, resource:OperationOutcome{...'Resource request returns no results.'}}]}. Naive `len(Bundle.entry)` reports 1, not 0.
headlines.integrator_impact: Match-counting code MUST filter Bundle.entry by `entry.search.mode != 'outcome'` and `resource.resourceType != 'OperationOutcome'`. Otherwise zero-result searches appear as one-result searches, and downstream loops process the OperationOutcome as if it were a clinical resource — silent data corruption.

citations.label: FHIR R4 § Bundle search entry mode

headlines.finding: Patient search refuses to enumerate without demographics-or-_id; birthdate alone fails
headlines.evidence: GET /Patient?birthdate=1973-06-03 → HTTP 200 with total=0. GET /Patient?birthdate=ge1990-01-01&birthdate=le1995-12-31 → HTTP 400 ('A single valid date is required for birthdate' AND 'This resource requires demographics or _id parameter for searching'). Demographics combo (family+given+birthdate+gender) is required to harvest unknown patients.
headlines.integrator_impact: Epic's Patient search is not population-enumerable on the public sandbox: `Patient?_count=200` cannot be used to discover unknown patients. Live customer integrations face the same gate — patient discovery is by-identifier or by-demographics, never by-population. Catalog known sandbox patient IDs out of band and drive testing from that roster.

headlines.finding: Observation.code on vital-signs uses LOINC codes outside the official us-core-vital-signs subset
headlines.evidence: Sandbox vitals Observations carry LOINC codes such as 85354-9 (Blood pressure panel), 55284-4 (Blood pressure systolic and diastolic) — both LOINC, but the us-core-vital-signs ValueSet (extensible binding) enumerates a narrower subset of vital-sign LOINCs. 7 of 7 observed top-level codes fell outside the bound subset; component.code (8480-6 systolic, 8462-4 diastolic) similarly outside.
headlines.integrator_impact: Vital-signs LOINC selection is wider than US Core's named subset — clients filtering by `code.coding.code IN us-core-vital-signs-VS` will reject conformant Epic responses. Match by `code.coding.system='http://loinc.org'` and validate semantically rather than against the closed subset.

citations.label: US Core 6.1 — Vital Signs Observation profile

headlines.finding: DocumentReference.type uses 100% Epic-proprietary OID codings; bound LOINC VS is unfilled
headlines.evidence: Sweep of 3 DocumentReference Bundles returned 3/3 type codings outside the bound LOINC-document-types VS. Sample codings: ('urn:oid:1.2.840.114350.1.72.727879.69848980', '1'), ('http://loinc.org', '11506-3'), ('urn:oid:1.2.840.114350.1.13.0.1.7.4.737880.5010', '1'). At least one LOINC code is mixed in alongside the Epic OIDs — but the binding is required, not extensible, so the OID-only paths violate.
headlines.integrator_impact: Document-type filtering by LOINC alone misses Epic-classified document types unless the Epic-OID-to-LOINC mapping is explicitly maintained. Required-binding violation is editorial-grade; Epic's own customers may have configured supplemental mappings. Verify with target deployment before assuming.

headlines.finding: Silent-param-ignore is 80% prevalent across P0 resources, not just Patient
headlines.evidence: Phase 3 sweep sent `?totally-bogus-param=foo&patient={id}` against 10 P0 resources. AllergyIntolerance, Condition, DiagnosticReport, DocumentReference, Encounter, Immunization, MedicationRequest, and Procedure all returned HTTP 200 + Bundle with valid data — the bogus param was silently dropped. Patient and Observation returned HTTP 400 OperationOutcome (severity=information for the bogus param plus a 'demographics required' fatal). The Bundle.link[self] URL still echoes the bogus param so the caller might infer 'they're filtering by it.'
headlines.integrator_impact: Typo'd or misspelled search params are accepted on 8/10 P0 resources without surfacing any error — clients get back data that looks filtered but isn't. The earlier v0.1 finding scoped this only to Patient; the multi-resource sweep shows it's the rule, not the exception. Always assert that the response actually reflects the intended filter (e.g. by re-checking that every entry has the queried category) rather than trusting the URL or status code.

citations.label: FHIR R4 § Search — handling unknown parameters

headlines.finding: Bulk Data $export endpoint returns IIS HTML 404, not a FHIR OperationOutcome
headlines.evidence: GET /$export?_outputFormat=application/fhir+ndjson with `Prefer: respond-async` and `Authorization: Bearer ...` returned HTTP 404 with text/html body ('<title>404 - File or directory not found.</title>'). Same IIS-not-FHIR-layer routing pattern as the 'FakeResourceTypeThatDoesNotExist' v0.1 finding — the URL never reaches Epic's FHIR application layer.
headlines.integrator_impact: System-level Bulk export at the FHIR base is not exposed on this Epic public sandbox endpoint, even with a broad `client_credentials` grant. Group-level (`/Group/{id}/$export`) likely requires a known Group ID, which the sandbox does not surface for enumeration. Integrations that need population data on this sandbox should pivot to per-patient compartment queries.

citations.label: FHIR Bulk Data Access (Flat FHIR) STU 2

headlines.finding: Reference fields are 100% relative-form on the sandbox; resolution rate is 100% over 30 samples
headlines.evidence: Phase 3 reference walk extracted 104 unique `Reference.reference` strings from the 33 v1 multi-patient golden files; 30 were sampled by GET. Every sample was `<ResourceType>/<id>` relative form (zero absolute URLs). Every sample resolved with HTTP 200; resourceType matched the path prefix in all cases.
headlines.integrator_impact: Clients can use simple URL concatenation (`{base}/{reference}`) without parsing absolute vs relative. Reference targets are stable on the sandbox — no broken-pointer 'integrator-trap' findings emerged from this probe.

headlines.finding: DocumentReference resources include R5-namespaced extensions on the R4 endpoint
headlines.evidence: HAPI's $validate against us-core-documentreference|6.1.0 (Phase 4 cross-check) reported on Camila Lopez's documents: 'Extension url http://hl7.org/fhir/5.0/StructureDefinition/extension-DocumentReference.attester is not valid (invalid Version)'. Epic emits R5-vintage extensions on resources delivered through the R4 endpoint.
headlines.integrator_impact: Strict R4 validators will reject these extensions. Defensive readers MUST tolerate unknown R5 extensions or namespace-mismatch-validate. Lossy parsers that strict-decode every extension URL will throw against Epic — common in FHIR libraries pinned to one major version's StructureDefinition catalog.

headlines.finding: Patient ombCategory race coding uses CDC OID 2.16.840.1.113883.6.238; HAPI without external terminology cannot validate
headlines.evidence: HAPI cross-validation flagged 'CodeSystem is unknown and can't be validated: urn:oid:2.16.840.1.113883.6.238' for Patient.extension(us-core-race).extension(ombCategory).valueCoding.system across all 3 sampled patients. The CDC Race & Ethnicity terminology referenced by us-core-omb-race-category VS does not ship in the IG package.
headlines.integrator_impact: Race/ethnicity membership validation requires the CDC terminology server (terminology.hl7.org) or a local mirror; stock HAPI/Inferno cannot resolve membership. Vendors emitting the OID literally are technically conformant but un-validatable with default tooling — a terminology-coverage gap, not a vendor bug.

headlines.finding: HAPI $validate agreed with the Map's four-axis structural analyzer on 31/38 cross-checked fixtures (82%)
headlines.evidence: Phase 4 cross-validation ran `HAPI $validate?profile=us-core-...` over 38 captured fixtures. 31 returned no error-severity issues from HAPI, matching the analyzer's `matches`/no-violation verdict; 7 disagreed on grounds documented in the related findings (R5 extensions on R4 endpoints, unknown CodeSystems, vendor OID codings). 13 fixtures were skipped (sweep summaries, error fixtures, and body-less responses).
headlines.integrator_impact: 82% agreement is a credible second-opinion signal. The seven disagreements cluster around two themes: terminology-coverage limitations (CDC OID, Epic OIDs, vendor OIDs in `DocumentReference.type`) and version-namespace drift (R5 extensions on R4). Both are real findings about how Epic deviates from a stock-tooling validator's expectations. The deviations are recorded as `element_deviations` rows; HAPI's per-fixture OperationOutcomes are preserved as `*-hapi-validation.json` golden files.

headlines.finding: Default content-type fallback is XML, not JSON, when Accept is omitted or unrecognized
headlines.evidence: Phase 6 content-type negotiation: Accept=application/fhir+json → 'application/fhir+json'; Accept=application/json → 'application/json'; Accept=application/xml → 'application/xml'; Accept=*/* → 'application/xml'; Accept omitted → 'application/xml'; Accept='this/is-not-a-real-mime' → 'application/xml'. Epic's content negotiation falls back to XML, not JSON, on unparseable or missing Accept headers.
headlines.integrator_impact: Many FHIR clients omit Accept (or send */*) and assume JSON because that's the modern norm. Epic will hand them XML, and the JSON parser will throw on the angle bracket. Always set Accept: application/fhir+json explicitly when targeting Epic — this is the most common cause of silent integration failures.

headlines.finding: Invalid bearer token returns RFC 6750-conformant 401 + WWW-Authenticate header
headlines.evidence: GET /Patient/{id} with Authorization: Bearer this-is-not-a-real-jwt → HTTP 401 with WWW-Authenticate: 'Bearer error="invalid_token", error_description="The access token provided is not valid"'. Concurrent token issuance (5 parallel JWT-bearer requests) returns 5 distinct access tokens (no caching/reuse).
headlines.integrator_impact: Token rotation works as expected and OAuth 2.0 RFC 6750 conformance is real (the WWW-Authenticate header carries enough information for a refresh-on-401 retry loop). No surprises here, but confirming this absence of surprises is itself Map data — many vendors do NOT populate WWW-Authenticate.

headlines.finding: No rate limiting observed at ~27 RPS over 30 requests
headlines.evidence: Phase 6 rate-limit probe fired 30 GET /Patient/{id} requests at concurrency=10 (~27.3 RPS). All 30 returned HTTP 200. No 429 responses, no Retry-After headers. Higher-volume probing (100+ RPS) was deliberately not run to avoid abuse of the public sandbox.
headlines.integrator_impact: Sub-30-RPS workloads are unlikely to hit rate limits on the sandbox; production tenants commonly cap at lower rates set by the customer's Epic admin. Don't assume the sandbox's lenience reflects production. If 429 ever surfaces in customer integration, capture the Retry-After value and update this finding.

verification.source_quote: See tests/golden/epic/phase-b-2026-04-26/ for the raw response files supporting these claims (12 v0.1 fixtures + 33 v1 multi-patient fixtures).
